data.title: The State Of REITs: December 2025 Edition
data.description:
REIT Performance

After a couple of tough months, the REIT sector rebounded in November with a +1.02% return. This narrows the year-to-date negative total return to -2.55% for the average REIT. REITs outperformed the broader market in November with average returns exceeding that of the Dow Jones Industrial Average (+0.5%), S&P 500 (+0.2%) and NASDAQ (-1.4%). The market cap weighted Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) more than doubled the return of the average REIT in November (+2.42% vs. +1.02%) and has handily outperformed year-to-date (+5.63% vs. -2.55%). The spread between the 2026 FFO multiples of large cap REITs (16.2x) and small cap REITs (12.8x) widened in November as multiples expanded 0.2 turns for large caps and 0.1 turns for small caps. Investors currently need to pay an average of 26.6% more for each dollar of FFO from large cap REITs relative to small cap REITs. In this monthly publication, I will provide REIT data on numerous metrics to help readers identify which property types and individual securities currently offer the best opportunities to achieve their investment goals.

In November mid cap (+3.53%), small cap (+3.38%) and large cap REITs (+0.32%) averaged gains, whereas micro caps (-8.76%) struggled. During the first eleven months of 2025, large cap REITs have outperformed small caps by 60 basis points.

9 out of 18 Property Types Averaged Positive Returns in November

Half of REIT property types averaged a positive total return in November. There was an enormous 33.18% total return spread between the best and worst performing property types. Data Centers (-10.86%) and Office (-6.06%) struggled in November, whereas Advertising (+22.32%) and Health Care (+8.50%) outpaced all other REIT property types.

Office (-18.35%), Land (-15.55%), Single Family Housing (-15.31%), Infrastructure (-14.17%), Data Centers (-13.93%) and Shopping Centers (-10.27%) have all averaged double-digit negative total returns over the first eleven months of 2025. Health Care (+30.53%) and Advertising (+24.67%) are the only property types to achieve double-digit positive returns year to date.

The REIT sector as a whole saw the average P/FFO (2026Y) increase from 13.5x to 13.7x during November. 50% of property types averaged multiple expansion, 44.4% averaged multiple contraction and 5.6% saw multiples hold steady. Data Centers (22.4x), Land (21.3x), Manufactured Housing (17.7x) and Shopping Centers (17.1x) currently trade at the highest average multiples among REIT property types. Hotels (7.8x) and Office (8.9x) are the only property types that average single digit FFO multiples.

Performance of Individual Securities

OUTFRONT Media (OUT) (+33.01%) led the REIT sector in November, surging after posting very strong Q3 earnings and raising guidance. After the strong November share price gain, OUT is now the 9th best performing REIT thus far in 2025.

Office Properties Income Trust (OPI) (-54.53%) continued to freefall throughout November after filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on October 30th. The brutal November performance brings OPI’s YTD total return to -98.25%.

68.15% of REITs had a positive total return in November. REITs have averaged a -2.55% year-to-date total return in 2025, which is 1300 basis points behind the +10.45% return for the REIT sector over the first 11 months of 2024.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Dividend Yield

Dividend yield is an important component of a REIT's total return. The particularly high dividend yields of the REIT sector are, for many investors, the primary reason for investment in this sector. As many REITs are currently trading at share prices well below their NAV, yields are currently quite high for many REITs within the sector. Although a particularly high yield for a REIT may sometimes reflect a disproportionately high risk, there exist opportunities in some cases to capitalize on dividend yields that are sufficiently attractive to justify the underlying risks of the investment. I have included below a table ranking equity REITs from highest dividend yield (as of 11/30/2025) to lowest dividend yield.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Although a REIT’s decision regarding whether to pay a quarterly dividend or a monthly dividend does not reflect on the quality of the company’s fundamentals or operations, a monthly dividend allows for smoother cash flow to the investor. Below is a list of equity REITs that pay monthly dividends ranked from highest yield to lowest yield.

Economic Health

Corporate bankruptcies in November declined month-over-month from October, but remain highly elevated. 2025 has already seen more bankruptcies in the first 11 months than any full year since 2010. With the Federal Reserve having just cut rates another 25 basis points at the December meeting and potentially continuing to cut throughout 2026, highly leveraged and financially strained companies may soon be able to refinance some of their high-interest rate debt at lower rates. This potential reduction in interest costs could help guide the number of corporate bankruptcies back downward in 2026 after a sharp upward spike in 2025.

Valuation

REIT Premium/Discount to NAV by Property Type

Below is a downloadable data table, which ranks REITs within each property type from the largest discount to the largest premium to NAV. The consensus NAV used for this table is the average of analyst NAV estimates for each REIT. Both the NAV and the share price will change over time, so I will continue to include this table in upcoming issues of The State of REITs with updated consensus NAV estimates for each REIT for which such an estimate is available.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Takeaway

The large cap REIT premium (relative to small cap REITs) widened in November and investors are now paying on average about 27% more for each dollar of 2026 FFO/share to buy large cap REITs than small cap REITs (16.2x/12.8x - 1 = 26.6%). As can be seen in the table below, there is presently a strong positive correlation between market cap and FFO multiple.

The table below shows the average NAV premium/discount of REITs of each market cap bucket. This data, much like the data for price/FFO, shows a strong, positive correlation between market cap and Price/NAV. The average large cap (-6.67%) and mid cap REIT (-8.48%) trade at single-digit discounts to consensus NAV. Small cap REITs (-24.19%) trade around 3/4 of NAV while micro caps (-31.33%) trade for a little over 2/3 of their respective NAVs.

Contrary to the REIT Sector’s Underperformance, REITs Continue to Achieve Growth at the Property Level

In Q3 2025 the median implied cap rate for REITs fell 10 basis points from Q2 and 48 basis points year-over-year down to 7.7%. The median implied REIT cap rate has decreased 5 quarters in a row, but remains elevated compared to pre-covid levels.

Median implied cap rates are highest among Hotel and Office REITs and lowest for Residential and Industrial REITs.

REIT median same-store occupancy ticked up to 94.4% in Q3 2025 rising from 94.2% in the 2nd quarter. Same-store occupancy has trended upward since bottoming out in 2020 due to government-imposed lockdowns. SS-NOI growth decelerated in Q3 but remains solidly positive.

SS-NOI growth trends vary among the big four property types with Industrial seeing the strongest growth followed by Retail and Residential. Office SS-NOI growth continues to trend downward falling deeper into negative territory in Q3.

In Q3 2025 REITs averaged 2.3% year-over-year SS-NOI growth led by Data Centers (+8.0%), Industrial (+5.2%), Health Care (+4.1%) and Shopping Centers (+4.1%). Office (-3.4%) and Self-storage (-2.0%), however, are currently suffering through negative SS-NOI growth.

Gladstone Land (LAND) (-20.4%) had the worst SS-NOI growth in Q3 followed by 5 Office REITs: Creative Media & Community Trust (CMCT) (-10.7%), Hudson Pacific Properties (-10.7%), Vornado Realty Trust (VNO) (-8.2%), Paramount Group (PGRE) (-8.0%) and Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT) (-7.8%).

NexPoint Diversified Real Estate Trust (NXDT) (+26.5%) saw the highest SS-NOI growth in the 3rd quarter while double-digit growth was also achieved by American Healthcare REIT (AHR) (+16.4%), Welltower (WELL) (+14.5%) and UMH Properties (UMH) (+12.1%). While high growth WELL and AHR trade at enormous NAV premiums and very high FFO/share multiples, UMH is in a very different position. UMH has much stronger growth than their Manufactured Housing peers Sun Communities (SUI) and Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS), but trades at a deeper NAV discount and an FFO multiple that is several turns lower. Although investors often need to pay a premium for growth, opportunities do emerge sometimes in which superior growth can be attained at a surprisingly favorable relative valuation.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Geron Corporation: Can Restructuring Bring The Stock Back To Life? I'm On The Sidelines
data.description:
Investment Overview

My last update on Geron (GERN) for Seeking Alpha was written in June, when shares in the commercial stage Pharma based in Foster City, California, traded at a value of $1.54, and its market cap was ~$1bn.

I covered in detail the company's journey to the approval of its "first in class telomerase inhibitor" Rytelo (imetelstat), which, according to Geron's Q3 10Q filing:

... harnesses Nobel Prize-winning science in a treatment that scientific evidence suggests reduces proliferation of malignant cells, allowing production of new healthy cells, which we believe drives differentiated clinical benefits, potentially altering the underlying course and modifying the disease of these hematologic malignancies.

Rytelo is approved to treat adult patients with low- to intermediate-1 risk myelodysplastic syndromes, or lower-risk MDS. Approval was secured in June last year, triggering a spike in Geron's share price to ~$5 per share.

As I discussed in my last note, however, the gains were rapidly eroded, and the stock has remained more or less stuck in a rut since, sinking as low as $1 per share, although it has found some momentum in the past month, up over 15%. The current traded price is $1.37, and the market cap is $875m.

Shares are up 7% in trading today, after Geron announced a strategic restructuring to "position the company for long-term value creation", which will involve cutting the workforce by one third. CEO Harout Semerjian, who was appointed four months ago, said in a statement:

After my first four months at Geron, the leadership team and I have assessed the business with the goal of streamlining our organizational structure to advance our strategy and create long-term value. We are implementing these changes from a position of strength and in the spirit of prudent fiscal management.

Our key objectives remain unchanged. We are focused on driving RYTELO commercial growth in the U.S., exploring opportunities for making RYTELO available outside the U.S., and continuing to advance our Phase 3 IMpactMF trial.

Analysis - Sluggish Sales Of Rytelo Undermining Progress

As we can see below, quarterly sales of Rytelo have not shown much growth in recent quarters:

I noted in my last post that Geron had announced its CEO of 14 years, John Scarlett, would be leaving the company, and I also noted the departure of Geron's Chief Commercial Officer ("CCO") in July 2024, and that it seemed an odd time to leave, given the commercial launch was about to begin.

A new CCO has been appointed, Ahmed ElNawawi, and it seems clear the new management team has taken actions to streamline operations, and stem losses, although we may note that Geron's nine-month net loss in 2025 was "only" $(54.6m), or $(0.08) per share, while its cash position stood at $421.5m as of the end of Q3.

Rytelo has a unique and differentiated mechanism of action ("MoA"), that has been demonstrated in studies to achieve durable red blood cell transfusion independence and response rates, with a generally manageable safety profile. In a recent presentation, management suggests that Rytelo is:

... positioned to be second-line treatment of choice on FDA label and NCCN Guidelines.

An overview of the market opportunity for Rytelo is also provided, as shown below (we should also note Rytelo has also secured approval in Europe, with plans to ship product to Germany in Q1 2026):

Geron says it has identified over 10k healthcare practitioners ("HCPs") treating MDS patients in the US, and its goal is to create more awareness of its drug within this community. Patent protection for the drug is apparently in place until 2037 in the US, so the company may stick with its premium price of ~$365k per annum, although the high price point has attracted some criticism.

I broadly agree with management that more awareness of its product and its unique advantages is necessary to drive sales, however the treatment regime for patients with MDS is complex - consider this analysis of the competition provided in Geron's 2024 annual report/10K submission:

In lower-risk MDS, RYTELO competes against a number of currently existing therapies, including ESAs and other hematopoietic growth factors that are indicated for anemia; immunomodulators, such as Revlimid (lenalidomide) by Celgene Corporation, or Celgene, a Bristol Myers Squibb Company, or BMS, company; hypomethylating agents, such as Vidaza (azacitidine) by Celgene and manufacturers of generic azacitidine; Dacogen (decitabine) by Otsuka America Pharmaceutical, Inc. and other manufacturers in the U.S. and Janssen in the EU; Inqovi (oral combination of decitabine and cedazuridine) by Astex Pharmaceuticals, Inc., or Astex; Tibsovo (ivosidenib), an IDH1 inhibitor, by Servier Pharmaceuticals, LLC; and Reblozyl (luspatercept), a TGF-beta inhibitor, by BMS. In August 2023, luspatercept was also approved for the treatment of anemia in ESA-naive adult patients with very low-to intermediate-risk MDS who may require regular RBC transfusions.

Other therapies currently in Phase 3 development in lower-risk MDS include elritercept (KER-050), a TGF-beta inhibitor, by Keros Therapeutics, Inc.; and Reblozyl (luspatercept) in non-transfusion-dependent lower-risk MDS patients, by BMS.

The fact that Geron continues to discuss Celgene - acquired by BMY in 2019 - may be a concern in itself, but more importantly, we can see that physicians have multiple treatment options to choose from, and in the below slide, we can see the complex guidelines they are provided with:

In summary, my concern for Geron going forward is whether it can truly gain entrenched market share in a confusing market where there are multiple different treatment approaches, and multiple "blockbuster" (+$1bn revenues per annum) drugs being marketed and sold by some of the world's largest Pharma companies.

Looking Ahead - What Might 2026 Have In Store For Geron?

On its Q3 earnings call with analysts, Geron management declined to provide any guidance for 2026, responding to an analysts' question on the subject as follows:

We are not giving any guidance at this point on our top line, but we look forward to that being something that we plan to tackle in the near future. So we're assessing at this point.

But we think now we've put our heads down, get to work. And in 2026, this is a growth story over there. But we're not giving any specific guidelines in terms of is it's going to start growing on March 31 or April 15.

Geron gave five presentations at the American Society of Hematology ("ASH") 2025 Annual Meeting which took place earlier this month, possibly accounting for some of the stock's recent gains.

The biggest catalysts for share price gains (other than stronger than expected sales) in 2026 that I can identify is the interim data from Geron's Phase 3, IMPACT-MF study in patients with myelofibrosis - a study overview is provided below:

Management believes approval in MF would double its market opportunity overnight, although Geron may face similar problems in this market as it does in MDS - quoting from the annual report again:

If approved for commercial sale for the treatment of relapsed/refractory MF, RYTELO would compete against currently approved JAK inhibitors: Jakafi (ruxolitinib) by Incyte Corporation, or Incyte, Inrebic (fedratinib) by Celgene, and OJJAARA (momelotinib), which was approved in September 2023 for the treatment of intermediate or high-risk MF, including primary MF or secondary MF (postpolycythemia vera and post-essential thrombocytopenia), in adults with anemia, by GlaxoSmithKline plc, or GSK, as well as a kinase inhibitor, Vonjo (pacritinib), by CTI Biopharma Corp., which was approved in February 2022 for the treatment of adults with Intermediate or High-Risk primary or secondary myelofibrosis with a platelet count below 50 × 109/L. Other treatment modalities for MF include hydroxyurea for the management of splenomegaly, leukocytosis, thrombocytosis and constitutional symptoms; splenectomy and splenic irradiation for the management of splenomegaly and co-existing cytopenias; chemotherapy; and pegylated interferon. Drugs for the treatment of MF-associated anemia include ESAs, androgens, danazol, corticosteroids, thalidomide and lenalidomide.

Whether you view the sheer number of different treatments as formidable, or bewildering, it does present a significant challenge for Geron commercially speaking, but there is no better way to enter the market than with a "best-in-class" profile, which the Phase 3 study might (emphasis on "might") demonstrate.

Geron suggests the median overall survival in its Phase 2 study "compares favourably to historical controls", and is "more than double compared to best available therapy ("BAT") although these are patients that discontinued therapy with ruxolitinb.

In summary, in terms of what to look out for in 2026, revenues, clinical progress, and cost management stand out - Geron is in no danger of exhausting its funding, and costs ought to be reduced in 2026 following the restructuring, although not by enough to stem net losses, I'd speculate.

Concluding Thoughts - Am I Making A Change To My Hold Call For 2026?

Short answer, no. It's clear that Geron has had a difficult year in 2025, struggling to grow revenues while leaking cash, and seeing revenues fall sequentially in Q3.

On the other hand, that is somewhat to be expected given the arrival of a new CEO and CCO, who undoubtedly need time to implement their new strategies. The market's reaction to the strategic restructuring announced today - sending the stock up over 7% - seems to suggest it favors the decision, although with fewer staff, how does Geron go about increasing awareness of its product?

My feeling is that it will be hard for Geron to drive increasing adoption of its only approved drug in such a crowded and complex market, unless it can show that Rytelo is a bona fide best-in-class candidate, but I am not sure the data exists to make that case strongly enough.

Such data may arrive when the Phase 3 myelofibrosis data arrives next year, which could give the stock a boost, although it won't arrive until Q3 at the earliest, and of course, it could undermine the investment case for Rytelo, as opposed to enhancing it.

As such, I am maintaining a watching brief - let's see what the revenue picture looks like when FY24 results are announced next year, and if management is able to provide some guidance? Even then, it may be a little early to judge the company. Rytelo has driven ~$136m of revenues in 2025 to the end of Q3 - by no means a bad number, and supportive of its current valuation, in my view, given this was the drug's first full year on the market.

The hard work starts here for new management, however - it has a good drug candidate to work with, but arguably not a best-in-class drug, which, unless management can prove otherwise, tells me I am better off on the sidelines - readers will undoubtedly have their own views on the matter.

data.title: U.S. Fed Cuts Rates By A Quarter Point
data.description:
The U.S. Federal Reserve has lowered its key interest rate by 25 basis points to a range of 3.5-3.75%. Scott Colbourne, Managing Director, Head of Active Fixed Income at TD Asset Management, speaks with MoneyTalk’s Anthony Okolie about the rate cut and the likelihood of more cuts to come in the year ahead.

Transcript

Antony Okolie: As expected, the US Federal Reserve lowered its key rate by a quarter point to a range between 3.5-3.75%. Joining me now to discuss is Scott Colbourne with TD Asset Management.

And Scott, did anything stand out for you today?

Scott Colbourne: Well, there was a couple of things that stood out to me. The Fed did cut rates 25 basis points, as everybody expected. But I would say there was a handful of things that really stood out. One is dissents. That hasn't happened in a long time.

Going into this, there was a consideration of as many as five dissents. We got three, one for 50 basis points of easing and two for, basically, the Fed on hold. So that is unusual and speaks to the tensions in terms of the dual mandate at the Fed.

The second thing was a surprise announcement on balance sheet reserve management. And they call it reserve management policy-- unexpected. The Fed had hinted that we were going to move towards-- the Fed had ended quantitative tightening December 1. Now there was a question of when they might start purchasing short-term instruments and add to reserves. And that came today, sooner than the market expected.

So there was that. And then, obviously, the Fed introduced this language called extent and timing of further adjustments is basically dependent on the data, and that there's a big-- when are we going to get a lot of the data is an important part of the direction of where the Fed goes after this. What I would characterize as the last of three risk management cuts that Governor Powell had acknowledged that the labor market was weakening. They did these three cuts. Now we're going to sit back, and we're going to have to deal with a divided Fed and see where we go from here.

Antony Okolie: And that's my question, because we have seen a lack of data. So what are the chances of a further cut next year, especially if we do see that inflation remains sticky and that that data is just not there?

Scott Colbourne: Yeah. I mean, we could be having this conversation next week because we're going to get two labor market data points next week and then another one by the time the Fed meets in January. And all of what we're talking about right now, including the SEP and all their forecasts, could change dramatically because of their emphasis on risk management towards labor markets.

So that in mind, at the moment, the SEP forecasts give us a sense that, basically, there's one cut priced in for next year and then the year after as we get close to the terminal. So, the leaning is that the Fed wants to continue its path towards neutral, which is ultimately 3%, and another cut each in the next two years.

At the margin, the markets are taking this as the combination of the reserve management policy and the cut and the slightly less-feared descent profile. It can be interpreted today as less hawkish than expectations were going into the meeting.

Antony Okolie: And what are US bond yields telling us about the Fed's next move? Because we see a lot of uncertainty around a change of leadership at the Fed that's going to happen next year. We've also got the Supreme Court's looming decision on tariffs. What is the bond market telling us right now?

Scott Colbourne: We've had an inflation market-- the inflation TIPS market-- giving it, basically, a steady range on inflation expectations. We've had a slight pricing-in of slightly higher-term premium in the longer end of the market. But basically, for the last little bit, markets have been in a bit of a range with a slight upward bias. The extent of the curve steepening-- that was a big theme this year in 2025-- has sort of abated in the last couple of months.

So basically, I think the markets are fairly priced for where we're going right now. But we're going to have a deluge of data. And as you acknowledge, there is going to be a big shift in the Fed. Obviously, Governor Powell's term is up, and his last meeting's in April. We'll have a new governor. And we have Lisa Cook, potentially, maybe off the Fed Board of Governors. So we've got lots of crosscurrents at play that could change dramatically in the first quarter of this year. But for the moment, this steadiness with a slight curve steepening bias.

Antony Okolie: So given that outlook and this latest decision, what's your outlook on the US dollar going forward?

Scott Colbourne: So I think when we look at what was supportive of the US dollar, it was this exceptionalism-- starting with, monetary policy was exceptional, an incredible fiscal tailwind. And then we've had the exceptional equity market and AI.

Monetary policy is less exceptional globally now. We've actually seen in recent weeks a shift in Reserve Bank of Australia and New Zealand, and even here at home, where the bond markets have started pricing out any further cuts and, in fact, started to look--

So, the US dollar is less exceptional. And so, one of the markets that have moved after today's meeting a little bit more is the US dollar is a little bit weaker. And I think that's a trend that will continue to see play out next year.

Antony Okolie: And finally, I want to talk about some of the things that you'll be watching closely over the next few weeks-- most likely weeks-- that could sway the Fed towards potential cuts in 2026.

Scott Colbourne: As I mentioned earlier, I'm starting next week with two labor force drops, and then another one in January. That will set the tone in terms of where we're going in the labor market. Obviously, there is Supreme Court rulings, IEEPA, Lisa Cook. Those are all very important for the composition and extent of a Fed that is divided, right?

I mean, we look at today's SEP, and there was actually six dissents-- what we call soft dissents. Six people, six governors, didn't want to cut. We only had three that were in the minutes and acknowledged.

So, there's a Fed divided. There's lots of tensions, lots of different outlooks. Governor Powell acknowledged that today. He said, look, there's a tension between inflation and labor market. And so, that is what we're going to have to resolve. And we're going to start with three very important data points into January's Fed meeting.

Original Post

data.title: Avoid The High-Yield Fund Trap: My Blueprint For High Yield And High Returns
data.description:
Retiring on passive income from dividends is a very attractive approach because it helps to simplify the investing process and to help you psychologically by enabling you to focus on your income stream as your true source of financial freedom and ability to retire from your investments rather than obsessing over your portfolio's current value, which changes dramatically with market volatility.

In addition, it can reduce the sequence of returns risk because dividends tend to be much less volatile than stock market returns, thereby making your stream of income to fund your living expenses much more dependable. This is particularly true given that current broad market indexes are at historically high valuations that have led some leading investors like Howard Marks to caution that we could be in for a lost decade ahead.

That being said, there are different approaches to generating passive income to fund living expenses in retirement. One popular approach is to build a cash flow stream from a portfolio of broadly diversified high-yield ETFs and CEFs that pay out attractive dividends month after month and do not need to generate much in the way of growth to still deliver attractive total returns. Another approach is to build a portfolio of individual stocks, bonds (BND), preferreds (PFF), as well as some strategically picked ETFs (SCHD) and CEFs (UTG) to still generate a similarly high yield, but also to use value investing principles and opportunistic capital recycling to maximize risk adjusted total returns over the long term along with strong dividend growth.

In this article, I will compare these two approaches and share why I think the latter approach is vastly superior to the former approach.

The High-Yield Fund Strategy: Pros and Cons

An investing strategy that focuses on high-yield funds does have a lot going for it. First of all, it is really simple because you do not have to do individual stock research, you do not have to listen to earnings calls, know how to do valuation models, track the volatility of your underlying holdings, worry about stepping on individual landmines, or any of that. Instead, you can simply look at funds that are broadly diversified already, and in some cases have professional management, make sure that they generate yields that are attractive to you, and especially when it comes to CEFs, you can implement a degree of value investing by looking at the current price relative to NAV as well as its historical price to NAV. All you have to do is try to avoid overpaying for these funds, and ultimately, between the income and hoping that eventually the price-to-NAV ratio will move in your favor, you should be able to generate a pretty attractive return over time. Additionally, you do not have to worry about proper portfolio diversification because these funds are already so well diversified, especially if you own several of them, that is all taken care of for you.

However, these funds do suffer from several drawbacks that are unique to them as opposed to the alternative strategy I mentioned. One of the biggest ones is the high expense ratios, as these funds are often actively managed, and in many cases charge 1% or more of assets under management as a fee to the management.

Additionally, many of these funds implement significant amounts of leverage that further increase their expense ratios through having to pay interest on their leverage, while also greatly increasing their risk since elevated leverage ratios mean that moves up and down in underlying equity prices are amplified. Not only that, in many cases these funds use margin, which means that if the market were to crash sharply, they could have a margin call, which would force them to sell shares at the bottom of the market, thereby locking in permanent losses and probably forcing a dividend cut.

In fact, in one case, there was a Nuveen-managed MLP CEF (JMF) that did not have particularly high leverage compared to other CEFs out there today, and it invested primarily in blue-chip MLPs (AMLP) like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), MPLX (MPLX), and Energy Transfer (ET). However, when the market crashed during COVID with energy prices plummeting, the fund's NAV plunged so sharply that it faced a margin call, and ultimately the fund had to shut down completely due to the losses, and so shareholders in that fund got crushed. This is an illustration of how quickly these sorts of things can happen.

The Problem of Return of Capital and Sequence Risk

Another big issue with this strategy is that many of these high-yield closed-end funds are not fully supporting their distributions with cash flow from their underlying holdings, and as a result, have to actually liquidate some of their underlying holdings to pay their distributions. While this can work as long as the market continues going up, it exposes you to a sequence of returns risk, which is exactly what this strategy is supposed to mitigate. If the fund's underlying holdings fall significantly and remain suppressed for a long period of time, you could end up locking in significant losses on a permanent basis by having to sell underlying holdings to support the distribution, thereby depleting the NAV of the fund over time. This means that, effectively, you are paying a higher expense ratio and, in some cases, taking on significant leverage risk to simply have a similar sequence of returns profile as if you were buying a low-cost S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and simply liquidating that over time to meet your living expenses. Therefore, with this approach, you are getting the worst of both worlds, as you are getting higher costs from active management and paying higher fees while not being able to mitigate your sequence of returns risk.

Additionally, some high-yield funds under this strategy generate their yield from selling calls (JEPI)(JEPQ)(QQQI)(SPYI). While this also can work out okay if the market at least remains flat or growing, it also can lead to NAV erosion over time because while the upside potential is capped from the covered calls, the downside potential is unlimited. Therefore, if the market were to crash and then rebound sharply, there is a potential that you could participate fully in the downside, but then your upside could be limited if the calls expire and get rewritten in the middle of that recovery period. Additionally, you are paying significantly higher expense ratios for these funds than you would for simply holding the underlying S&P 500 (VOO) or NASDAQ (QQQ) index that they track. Once again, this often boils down to you paying higher fees to have some of your capital returned to you effectively over time rather than just liquidating your own portfolio to fund your living expenses over time with a much lower expense ratio.

Another big risk that these funds can bring to the table is that their underlying holdings may be of very low quality, which enables them to generate the high yields in the first place. For example, if you load up on highly leveraged funds that invest in junk-rated debt (JNK) or, in some cases, mortgage REITs (MORT) or high-yield, high-risk securities, I am sure you have some diversification that does provide a degree of security. Ultimately, if the economy goes down, these securities are likely to suffer tremendously too, which could lead to huge capital losses and painful dividend cuts.

Ultimately, no matter how you slice it, these are all major cons to this approach, and perhaps the worst one of all is that you are loading up on holdings that are either of bad quality or employ some form of financial engineering to generate the distributions. That means that your sequence of return risk is really no better than if you were to simply invest in a low-cost diversified ETF, yet you are paying much higher fees and, in some cases, take on significant leverage risk to do so. At the very least, you are simply holding probably diversified portfolios of securities that, while they may pay out high yields that support the fund's distribution, and they may even be somewhat quality and defensive, they are poor long-term compounders because of their lack of strong underlying growth. Additionally, by paying higher expense ratios for these funds, you are further reducing the total return proposition, and since they are large and diversified, they tend not to implement effective ongoing value investing principles that could be used to accelerate the compounding process.

Why My Value Investing Approach Is Superior

So why is my approach better? First of all, it does not implement leverage. It does not invest much, if at all, in high-cost funds, so it does not have expense ratios eroding gains, and the passive income it generates from investments is fully generated by the holdings that are supported by cash flows, so therefore they are much more doable, durable, and truly separated from the stock market price performance in terms of sequence of returns risk. It also does not use covered calls to generate income, further reducing market volatility and sequence of returns risk.

Perhaps best of all, though, is the fact that by focusing on individual stocks, bonds, and preferreds, and only selectively using ETFs and CEFs for diversification purposes and to take advantage of opportunistic market conditions, I can overlay a value investing and opportunistic capital recycling approach to accelerate the compounding process. Essentially, what this boils down to is by focusing on mid- to high-yielding dividend stocks that have high-quality balance sheets and business models and solid growth potential over time, I can value these securities with a much higher degree of conviction than I would be able to with a broadly diversified fund with scores or even hundreds of underlying holdings or some high-growth, high-valuation, multiple, low-to-no-yield tech stock (XLK).

Therefore, I have a much higher degree of confidence that I am either buying it at an attractive valuation or selling it at around fair value. I can then more effectively implement opportunistic capital recycling to buy stocks on the cheap, sell them when they reach fair value, and rinse and repeat to accelerate the compounding process. This way, I can turn high single-digit to low double-digit annualized total returns into mid- to high-teens annualized total returns and dramatically accelerate dividend growth as well by recycling capital from lower-yielding stocks that have reached fair value to higher-yielding undervalued alternatives.

Investor Takeaway

While the approach of buying a basket of high-yield funds and letting the cash distributions serve as the total return profile with little to no need for growth certainly sounds simple and appealing, as this article points out, it has numerous major flaws with it. Instead, by implementing value investing principles to select individual stocks, bonds, and preferreds while also complementing that with a select few funds, opportunistically, investors can put their portfolio on a much firmer foundation while generating a very attractive high single-digit current yield and significant dividend growth and total returns over time. This is what we have been able to do to outperform the market over time in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Post-FOMC Weakness: U.S. Dollar Breakdown Continues After 25bps Cut
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The US Dollar took a hit following yesterday's Fed decision, driven by mechanical flows typical after a rate cut.

Given the rally to new cycle highs over the past two months, current outflows aren't surprising—benefiting not just the majors, but especially exotic currencies.

Prior to the meeting, the Dollar had held relatively strong despite a dovish Fed repricing, ranging near its recent highs supported by elevated yields following Williams' comments.

However, despite the Dot Plot projecting only ~2 cuts for 2026 and Chair Powell suggesting the US is entering the "high end of the neutral rate range," the Dollar is sliding back to mid-October levels.

The cut itself was neither explicitly dovish nor hawkish; communications were solid.

If you missed Powell's speech (great recap right here), he placed extra emphasis on inflation. Consequently, as noted yesterday, future inflation data may carry even more weight than the NFP (which is projected to ease gradually over the next year).

The Fed has been driving blind with limited inflation reports since September due to the government shutdown, making upcoming prints critical for markets.

November NFP is scheduled for December 16, followed by CPI on Dec 18.

Let's dive into the Dollar Index (DXY) charts to spot how much deeper the Dollar can correct given this shift in fundamentals.

Dollar Index (DXY) Multi-timeframe Outlook

Daily Chart

The Dollar is falling harshly after trying to hold its elevated range, nothing too surprising here – Yesterday's move was based from its 200-Day Moving Average acting as resistance and now breaking below its key Pivot Area rendering the DXY bearish short-term.

Looking back even further, the US Dollar is holding a long-term rangebound trajectory since mid-2025 between 97.00 to 100.00 – Makes sense when looking at its first-half harsh fall (-12% since January).

On the bigger picture, cuts have been expected for a long-time in the US so yesterday did not surprise Macro traders too much.

Still, with the Daily RSI going towards the bearish territory and momentum getting strong, the fall should continue at least towards the 98.00 Support area, allowing other majors to appreciate.

4H Chart and Technical Levels

Levels to place on your DXY charts:

Resistance Levels

  • 100.00 to 100.50 Main resistance zone
  • 100.376 November highs
  • 99.80 mini-resistance
  • 98.50 to 98.80 Pivot Zone

Support Levels

  • 98.25 Lower bound of 4H Channel
  • 98.00 Key support (+/- 100 pips) Next support
  • 97.40 to 97.80 August Range Support
  • 2025 Lows 96.40 to 96.80 Support

1H Chart

Watch to the reactions as the Dollar reaches the lower bound of the Channel between 98.20 to 98.30 and spot how other assets correlate.

There has been profit-taking flows overnight in Equities so keep an eye on this and flows in other FX currencies throughout the session.

Safe Trades!

data.title: Silver Steadies With Buyers Defending Momentum In Supply-Driven Rally (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Silver pushed deeper into record territory this week, holding above $62 per ounce on Thursday as traders absorbed the Federal Reserve’s latest policy signals and a softer dollar reduced the cost of holding precious metals. Chair Jerome Powell’s remarks leaned more dovish than expected, even as guidance on future rate cuts stayed cautious.

That was enough to trigger another extension in a market already supported by tightening physical conditions and rising investment flows. Physical market behavior has become a defining factor. Borrowing costs and lease rates have risen, signaling genuine delivery stress rather than speculative positioning pressure. That dynamic gives the current rally a firmer foundation than previous cycles, with industrial and investment forces aligning more tightly than at any point this year.

Technical structure remains one of the strongest in commodities

The daily chart shows a steep and orderly uptrend. Silver is trading comfortably above the 20, 50, 100 and 200-day EMAs, with each moving average sloping higher. The 20-day EMA near $56 has served as the primary launchpad for the breakout, and every dip this quarter has found buyers sooner than the one before it.

Silver price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

Momentum reflects the same strength. RSI has pushed into the upper-70s, an overbought region that typically signals trend continuation rather than weakness when fundamentals remain aligned. The pattern mirrors the earlier surge seen this year, only now supported by deeper physical constraints and stronger macro inflows.

Short-term action on the 30-minute chart reinforces bullish control. The Supertrend indicator remains positive, and SAR dots are attempting to shift back beneath price, a formation often associated with renewed intraday upside. Pullbacks into the $61.3–$61.4 region have been absorbed quickly, including after the most recent retreat from $62.5. As long as the Supertrend base at $61.3 holds, intraday rhythm favors buyers.

Macro forces and structural demand deepen silver’s rally

The broader narrative has tilted more decisively in silver’s favor. Inflation expectations have stabilized while real rates have stopped rising, and a softer dollar following the Fed’s announcement adds further support. Safe-haven flows have also re-emerged amid lingering geopolitical risks, reinforcing silver’s role within both the defensive and monetary-sensitive asset classes.

Industrial demand remains the most powerful structural force. The energy and data transition continues to reshape global consumption patterns. Solar panel production remains one of the largest and fastest-growing consumers of physical silver, while electric vehicles and server-farm components have expanded their footprint within the metal’s supply chain.

Independent surveys show deficits becoming structural rather than cyclical, with mine supply unable to keep pace as ore grades fall and new projects face multi-year delays.This combination of macro support, supply stress and industrial tightness has pushed silver into a self-reinforcing trend. Markets are now treating the metal not only as a monetary hedge but as a critical resource whose long-term equilibrium price may still be rising.

If momentum holds, silver could test $63.5 in the coming sessions, with $65 emerging as the next psychological marker. Only a break below $60.5 would signal fatigue, though deeper moving averages remain widely supportive.

Outlook as silver builds on its breakout

Silver continues to trade like a market with unfinished upside. Policy shifts have reduced headwinds, physical constraints are deepening, and industrial demand remains robust. Unless liquidity conditions shift abruptly or the dollar stages a sharp reversal, the bias stays pointed higher.

Previously, we discussed how the alignment of industrial demand, tight physical supply and macro inflows created a unique setup for silver to outperform other precious metals. The current expansion above $61 confirms that dynamic, with the breakout supported by both structural and cyclical tailwinds.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.
